items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What is corporate governance, and how does it differ from CSR?
items.correct: Corporate governance is the system of rules, practices and processes by which a company is directed and controlled — focusing on accountability, board structure and shareholder rights. CSR is about wider social/environmental responsibility.
items.misconception: Corporate governance is the same as corporate social responsibility.
items.random_wrong: Corporate governance is the legal requirement to have a CEO.
items.why: Governance is about how the company is run (board, audit, transparency); CSR is about what the company does for society. Students conflate them — both concern ethics but at different levels.

items.q: When might an autocratic leadership style be appropriate?
items.correct: In crises requiring quick decisions, when workers are inexperienced, or in highly hierarchical contexts — though it can damage morale long-term.
items.misconception: Autocratic leadership is always wrong and democratic leadership is always right.
items.random_wrong: Autocratic leaders are dictators who break the law.
items.why: Students moralise leadership styles. The business studies view is contingency-based — each style has appropriate contexts. Democratic is not universally best; autocracy works in emergencies.
Correct answer: Data you collect yourself. “Data from old books” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A small group giving opinions is a…
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Focus group. “Boardroom” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: 200 people were asked; 50 liked pizza. What percent liked pizza?
items.why: The real figure is 25. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
